title: Die Sternthaler
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 2, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 153. Text from Wikisource („Die Sternthaler (1857)“, twice proofread against the original). Fresh translation made directly from the German; 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The one hundred and fifty-third tale (KHM 153), under its 1857 title Die Sternthaler. An orphan girl with nothing but the clothes on her back gives them all away, piece by piece, to those poorer still, and is rewarded when the stars fall as silver coins into her shift. Older spelling is preserved (Sternthaler/Thaler, gieng, hungerig, Linnen).

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein kleines Mädchen, dem war Vater und Mutter gestorben, und es war so arm, daß es kein Kämmerchen mehr hatte darin zu wohnen und kein Bettchen mehr darin zu schlafen und endlich gar nichts mehr als die Kleider auf dem Leib und ein Stückchen Brot in der Hand, das ihm ein mitleidiges Herz geschenkt hatte.
sentences.l: There was once a little girl whose father and mother had died, and it was so poor that it no longer had a little chamber to live in nor a little bed to sleep in, and at last nothing at all any more but the clothes on its body and a little piece of bread in its hand, which a compassionate heart had given it.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a little girl whose father and mother had died, and she was so poor that she no longer had a little room to live in nor a little bed to sleep in, and at last nothing left but the clothes on her body and a little piece of bread in her hand that some compassionate soul had given her.
sentences.n: German “Mädchen” is grammatically neuter, so the pronouns referring to the girl are all neuter (dem, es, ihm, sein) throughout the tale — literally ‘it,’ though we say ‘she.’ “dem war Vater und Mutter gestorben” is a dative of the person affected: ‘to whom father and mother had died.’ The diminutives “Kämmerchen, Bettchen, Stückchen” (little chamber / bed / piece) set the tender, pitiable tone.

sentences.t: Es war aber gut und fromm.
sentences.l: But it was good and pious.
sentences.i: But she was good and pious.
sentences.n: “fromm” (pious, devout) is the keynote of the tale: the girl’s reward comes from her piety and charity. This religious framing runs all through the story.

sentences.t: Und weil es so von aller Welt verlassen war, gieng es im Vertrauen auf den lieben Gott hinaus ins Feld.
sentences.l: And because it was thus forsaken by all the world, it went, trusting in the dear God, out into the field.
sentences.i: And because she was thus forsaken by all the world, she went out into the open country, trusting in the dear Lord.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (went) — you meet it constantly in these editions. “im Vertrauen auf” = ‘in reliance/trust upon.’ “der liebe Gott” (the dear God/Lord) is the ordinary German way of naming God warmly.

sentences.t: Da begegnete ihm ein armer Mann, der sprach „ach, gib mir etwas zu essen, ich bin so hungerig.“
sentences.l: There a poor man met it, who spoke: “ah, give me something to eat, I am so hungry.”
sentences.i: There a poor man met her and said, “Ah, give me something to eat, I am so hungry.”
sentences.n: “begegnete ihm” takes the dative (ihm = ‘to it/her’): one meets *to* someone in German. “hungerig” is an older form of hungrig (hungry).

sentences.t: Es reichte ihm das ganze Stückchen Brot und sagte „Gott segne dirs“ und gieng weiter.
sentences.l: It handed him the whole little piece of bread and said “God bless it for you” and went further.
sentences.i: She handed him the whole little piece of bread and said, “God bless it to you,” and went on.
sentences.n: “Gott segne dirs” = “Gott segne dir es,” a set blessing: ‘may God bless it to you’ (the dative dir + contracted es). These pious formulas recur through the tale.

sentences.t: Da kam ein Kind das jammerte und sprach „es friert mich so an meinem Kopfe, schenk mir etwas, womit ich ihn bedecken kann.“
sentences.l: There came a child that lamented and spoke: “it freezes me so at my head, give me something with which I can cover it.”
sentences.i: There came a child that was wailing and said, “I am so cold on my head; give me something to cover it with.”
sentences.n: “es friert mich” is an impersonal construction, literally ‘it freezes me’ = ‘I am cold.’ “womit” = wo + mit, ‘with which.’ “ihn” (masc.) refers back to Kopf (head, masc.).

sentences.t: Da that es seine Mütze ab und gab sie ihm.
sentences.l: There it took its cap off and gave it to him.
sentences.i: So she took off her cap and gave it to him.
sentences.n: “that … ab” is the 1857 spelling of tat … ab, from abthun/abtun (to take off) — a separable verb, the prefix “ab” jumping to the end. Modern German drops the h: tat.

sentences.t: Und als es noch eine Weile gegangen war, kam wieder ein Kind und hatte kein Leibchen an und fror: da gab es ihm seins: und noch weiter, da bat eins um ein Röcklein, das gab es auch von sich hin.
sentences.l: And when it had gone a while longer, there came again a child and had no little bodice on and was cold: so it gave it its own: and still further on, there one begged for a little skirt, that it gave away too.
sentences.i: And when she had gone a while longer, another child came who had no little vest on and was cold: so she gave it hers; and further on still, one begged for a little frock, and that she gave away as well.
sentences.n: The escalation — cap, bodice, frock, and finally shirt — is the heart of the tale: she gives everything away. “Leibchen” is a little bodice/undervest; “seins” = ‘its (own one),’ the neuter possessive standing for the garment she gives. “von sich hin geben” = to give away from oneself.

sentences.t: Endlich gelangte es in einen Wald, und es war schon dunkel geworden, da kam noch eins und bat um ein Hemdlein, und das fromme Mädchen dachte „es ist dunkle Nacht, da sieht dich niemand du kannst wohl dein Hemd weg geben,“ und zog das Hemd ab und gab es auch noch hin.
sentences.l: At last it reached a forest, and it had already grown dark, there came yet one more and begged for a little shirt, and the pious girl thought: “it is dark night, no one sees you there, you can well give your shirt away,” and drew the shirt off and gave it away too.
sentences.i: At last she reached a forest, and it had already grown dark, when one more came and begged for a little shirt, and the pious girl thought, “It is dark night, no one can see you here, you may well give your shirt away,” and she pulled off the shirt and gave that away too.
sentences.n: “Hemdlein” is the diminutive of Hemd (shirt/shift) — here the child’s undergarment. Giving away even her last shirt in the dark, unseen, is the final proof of her selfless piety, rewarded in the next lines. “wohl” here softens the thought: ‘you may well / surely can.’

sentences.t: Und wie es so stand und gar nichts mehr hatte, fielen auf einmal die Sterne vom Himmel, und waren lauter harte blanke Thaler: und ob es gleich sein Hemdlein weg gegeben, so hatte es ein neues an und das war vom allerfeinsten Linnen.
sentences.l: And as it stood thus and had nothing at all any more, all at once the stars fell from the sky, and were nothing but hard shining thalers: and though it had given its little shirt away, yet it had a new one on, and that was of the very finest linen.
sentences.i: And as she stood there with nothing left at all, all at once the stars fell from the sky, and were nothing but hard, shining thalers; and although she had given her little shirt away, she now had a new one on, and it was of the very finest linen.
sentences.n: The “Sternthaler” (star-thalers) that give the tale its name: a Thaler was a large silver coin. “lauter” = ‘nothing but, sheer.’ “ob es gleich … weg gegeben” is an older concessive, ‘although it had given away’ (obgleich split around the pronoun). “Linnen” is the older spelling of Leinen (linen).

sentences.t: Da sammelte es sich die Thaler hinein und war reich für sein Lebtag.
sentences.l: There it gathered the thalers up into it and was rich for its life-day.
sentences.i: So she gathered the thalers up into it and was rich for the rest of her life.
sentences.n: “sich … hinein sammelte” — she gathers the coins into her new shift, held out like an apron. “für sein Lebtag” = ‘for its lifetime,’ a fixed old phrase, ‘for the rest of her days.’
